body:
{
  "title": "Nova Ramirez",
  "compiled_truth": "Nova Ramirez is the founder and CEO of [Umbra Storage](companies/umbra-storage), a data infrastructure company she started in early 2026 after spending nearly a decade at various infrastructure and developer-tools startups. She's known in the ecosystem as someone who thinks in multi-year arcs — the kind of person who'll sketch out a five-year roadmap on a napkin and then actually execute it. Colleagues describe her as "annoyingly patient" in a world that rewards speed above all else.

Before founding Umbra, Nova held senior engineering roles at two mid-stage infrastructure companies (neither of which survived past 2025, which she's candid about). She's talked openly about how watching those companies die shaped her philosophy: build for durability first, growth second. That mindset permeates Umbra's culture and its technical decisions.

Nova's technical depth is genuine — she still reviews architecture docs personally and has been known to push back on her own engineering leads when she thinks a design is too clever for its own good. She has a particular obsession with deterministic systems and has spoken at length about how non-determinism in data pipelines is the root cause of most operational nightmares. This philosophical alignment is part of what brought her into conversation with the [Anchor Codec](companies/anchor-codec) team in late 2027, when both companies began exploring whether their primitives could be complementary.

She's a quiet presence at conferences — rarely on the main stage but always in the hallway track, often surrounded by a small knot of engineers asking pointed questions. People who've worked with her say she has an unusual ability to hold two contradictory ideas in tension without rushing to resolve them, which makes her frustrating in meetings but effective over time.

Ramirez raised a modest seed round in mid-2026 and has been deliberatly conservative about hiring, keeping the team under twenty people through most of 2027. She's said publicly that she'd rather have twelve engineers who understand the full stack than forty who each own a slice. Whether that approach scales remains an open question, but so far Umbra's retention numbers suggest people like working there.",
  "timeline": "- **2026-02-14** | Nova Ramirez incorporates [Umbra Storage](companies/umbra-storage) in Delaware, begins recruiting founding engineering team.
- **2026-06-03** | Closes a $4.2M seed round led by a small infrastructure-focused fund. Keeps the announcement low-key — a blog post and nothing else.
- **2026-09-18** | Ships Umbra's first internal alpha to three design partners. Nova personally onboards each one.
- **2027-01-22** | Gives a hallway talk at a systems infrastructure meetup on "Why Determinism Matters More Than Performance" — it circulates widely on engineering blogs.
- **2027-05-11** | Hires Dara Okonkwo as VP of Engineering, Umbra's first non-IC senior hire.
- **2027-08-30** | Umbra launches public beta. Uptake is slow but retention among early users is unusually high.
- **2027-11-09** | First meeting with [Anchor Codec](companies/anchor-codec) leadership to discuss potential integration points between Umbra's storage layer and Anchor's single-pass primitives.
- **2028-01-17** | Nova presents at a closed-door infrastructure founders dinner, argues that the industry is over-indexed on throughput and under-indexed on correctness.
- **2028-04-22** | Umbra announces a $16M Series A. Team still under twenty-five people.
- **2028-07-03** | Joint technical design session with Anchor Codec engineering team in Portland. Nova attends in person."
}
